title: Ransomware Uses AI to Amp Up Negotiations
gist: Tom Uren's Seriously Risky Business newsletter argues sophisticated ransomware groups like FulcrumSec and DragonForce now use AI mainly to amplify extortion leverage in negotiations rather than to hack more, even as an AI-driven vendor 'patch frenzy' (570+ Microsoft CVEs this month) fails to translate into real-world security gains for under-resourced organizations.

themes.theme: AI as a negotiation weapon, not just a hacking tool

insights.claim: The author's central thesis is that sophisticated ransomware operators increasingly use AI not to hack more companies but to analyze stolen data and generate leverage that extracts larger payments in extortion negotiations, while less sophisticated actors mainly use AI just to hack more often.
insights.speaker: Tom Uren
insights.so_what: Reframes the ransomware-AI risk for security buyers: the bigger financial threat from top-tier actors is smarter negotiation and PR pressure, not just faster breach volume.
insights.quote: nerdy, technically focused ransomware actors use AI to hack, but sophisticated ransomware actors? They use it to negotiate
insights.locator: Our take-home message here is that nerdy, technically focused ransomware actors use AI to hack

insights.claim: FulcrumSec, a data extortion group active since roughly September 2025, uses simple intrusion techniques (hardcoded/exposed credentials, unpatched applications, misconfigured storage) but has breached 25 organizations and stolen several terabytes of data, then uses AI specifically to analyze that data and set a firm negotiating position.
insights.speaker: Tom Uren
insights.so_what: Shows the entry bar for large-scale, AI-augmented extortion is low — basic credential hygiene, not AI defense, remains the primary control.
insights.quote: We know what we have taken, here it is, and this is why we have set the ransom at this amount.
insights.locator: A GuidePoint Security report published last week detailed FulcrumSec's use of AI

insights.claim: In its June 2026 breach of Danish pharma company Novo Nordisk (maker of Wegovy and Semaglutide), FulcrumSec claimed to have stolen 1.3 terabytes across 700,717 files, including five undisclosed drug programs and private AI models for drug discovery, used a team of AI agents to analyze the stolen models, claimed the data could save competitors three to five years of development time, and set an initial ransom demand of $25 million.
insights.speaker: Tom Uren
insights.so_what: A concrete, high-value case showing AI-augmented data-extortion economics: attackers now quantify and monetize stolen R&D/IP value directly, raising tail risk for pharma and biotech IP.
insights.locator: In June this year, FulcrumSec reached out to DataBreaches[dot]Net regarding its compromise of Danish pharmaceutical company Novo Nordisk

insights.claim: FulcrumSec described Novo Nordisk's security posture as 'absolutely catastrophic' and framed its pitch in language likely to attract class-action litigation, and separately claimed it used an OpenAI API key stolen from a victim to pay for ChatGPT to summarize that victim's own stolen data.
insights.speaker: Tom Uren
insights.so_what: Extortion groups are now engineering breach disclosures to trigger follow-on legal liability for victims, compounding direct ransom costs with litigation exposure.
insights.locator: The information about the IP FulcrumSec stole was coupled with a description of Novo Nordisk's security posture

insights.claim: FulcrumSec builds pressure by producing polished, AI-assisted breach reports — complete with logos, file breakdowns, and stated motives — for threat researchers and journalists, as it did after breaching technology company Avnet in October 2025, when it gave the vx-underground X account a full autobiography-style writeup of the intrusion.
insights.speaker: Tom Uren
insights.so_what: AI-generated PR packaging is becoming a standard extortion tactic to maximize reputational damage and press coverage as leverage against victims.
insights.quote: every bit of research and write up for me
insights.locator: vx-underground said the group had done "every bit of research and write up for me."

insights.claim: The DragonForce ransomware group, active in various forms since 2023, uses LLMs to manufacture plausible psychological pressure in negotiations, including falsely claiming to have legal counsel on staff to imply insight into a victim's reporting requirements and legal exposure.
insights.speaker: Tom Uren
insights.so_what: Ransomware negotiation is becoming an AI-assisted psychological warfare exercise, which should shift victim-side negotiation playbooks (e.g., engaging specialized counsel/negotiators earlier).
insights.locator: the DragonForce ransomware group, around in various forms since 2023, is using LLMs to "manufacture plausible

themes.theme: AI-enabled hacking that fails to monetize

insights.claim: Earlier AI-enabled hacking cases were disconnected from monetization: a single hacker breached nine Mexican government agencies within weeks using various LLMs, and another hacker in Ethiopia breached at least 14 companies in partially automated attacks — in both cases the intrusion succeeded but converting access into cash failed.
insights.speaker: Tom Uren
insights.so_what: AI clearly lowers the technical bar for breaching organizations, but the extortion/monetization layer remains a separate, harder skill — capability alone doesn't equal financial threat.
insights.locator: In April we wrote about a single hacker who breached nine Mexican government agencies

insights.claim: Security firm Sysdig's JADEPUFFER malware was described as 'agentic ransomware' — a complete extortion operation driven end-to-end by an LLM — that executed the technical hacking well but had broken extortion mechanics: it encrypted files without storing or transmitting decryption keys (making recovery impossible), used the example Bitcoin address from Bitcoin's own documentation for ransom payment, and listed a contact email never before seen in threat-intel databases.
insights.speaker: Tom Uren
insights.so_what: Fully autonomous, end-to-end LLM-run ransomware is technically real but currently unreliable at the business-execution layer — a useful marker for how far 'agentic' cybercrime has actually matured.
insights.quote: agentic ransomware: a complete extortion operation driven end-to-end by a LLM
insights.locator: security firm Sysdig reported early this month on what it called JADEPUFFER

insights.claim: Last month's FortiBleed campaign, linked to the INC and Lynx ransomware groups, was an AI-driven hacking operation that used credential harvesting to feed data extortion campaigns, illustrating a strategy of using AI to hack more often to compensate for falling data-extortion payment rates.
insights.speaker: Tom Uren
insights.so_what: One viable ransomware strategy is volume (hack more to offset lower per-victim payouts); the other is leverage (extract more per victim via AI-amplified pressure) — both are AI-driven but structurally different threats.
insights.locator: Last month's so-called FortiBleed campaign, which has been linked to the INC and Lynx ransomware groups

insights.claim: Data extortion is becoming the preferred ransomware business model over file encryption because it is easier and quieter to execute, and because improving organizational backup strategies have reduced encryption's payoff even as data-extortion payment rates are simultaneously falling.
insights.speaker: Tom Uren
insights.so_what: A structural shift in ransomware economics: security spend on backups is working against encryption-based attacks, pushing the whole industry toward data theft and reputational leverage instead.
insights.locator: Data extortion is becoming the preferred business model because it is easier to carry out and quieter than encrypting ransomware

insights.claim: Microsoft's July 2026 Patch Tuesday addressed 570 vulnerabilities (about 10% rated critical), on top of roughly 50 more patched earlier in the month, up sharply from about 200 patched the prior month; Google separately patched 428 non-Microsoft Chromium vulnerabilities to be ported to Edge, and Adobe moved to a twice-monthly patch cycle, patching 88 vulnerabilities so far this month.
insights.speaker: Tom Uren
insights.so_what: A sharp, quantifiable spike in disclosed vulnerabilities across major vendors signals AI-assisted vulnerability discovery is now materially outpacing prior patch cadences — relevant leading indicator for enterprise patch-management workload and vendor security-tooling demand.
insights.locator: This month's Microsoft Patch Tuesday addressed 570 vulnerabilities

insights.claim: The author's contrarian view is that the AI-driven surge in vulnerability discovery and patching will not meaningfully reduce real-world risk, because many organizations never apply patches, and the same AI capabilities that find and fix bugs can be used by attackers to reverse-engineer patches into exploits — meaning the well-resourced get safer while the majority who patch slowly or not at all become relatively more exposed.
insights.speaker: Tom Uren
insights.so_what: A bearish read on 'AI is cleaning up cybersecurity debt' narratives: patch volume is not the same as risk reduction, and the security gap between well-resourced and under-resourced organizations is set to widen, not narrow.
insights.locator: Unfortunately, here at Risky Business we're not known for our optimistic worldview.

insights.claim: The author expects coding agents are 'YOLOing' the vast majority of new code being written today without regard for security, meaning the current patch avalanche addresses legacy technical debt but won't offset a fresh wave of insecure AI-generated code.
insights.speaker: Tom Uren
insights.so_what: Even as AI helps clear old vulnerabilities, it may simultaneously be generating new ones at scale — a durable, structural tailwind for application-security tooling vendors.
insights.quote: coding agents are YOLOing the vast majority of new code being written today, without giving a rat's ass about being secure
insights.locator: we expect coding agents are YOLOing the vast majority of new code being written today

themes.theme: Global cyber enforcement and policy notes

insights.claim: Interpol's Operation First Light, an anti-fraud action spanning 97 countries targeting social engineering scams and associated money laundering, intercepted $293 million in illicit assets and resulted in the arrest of over 5,800 individuals — described as the largest arrest count from a single operation the author can recall.
insights.speaker: Tom Uren
insights.so_what: One of the largest coordinated global fraud enforcement actions to date — a positive data point for cross-border law-enforcement capacity against scam/money-laundering networks.
insights.locator: Interpol has announced that Operation First Light, an anti-fraud action involving 97 countries

insights.claim: The EU and U.K. issued their first joint cyber sanctions, targeting Russian state and criminal cyber actors over December 2025 attacks on Poland's energy grid attributed to the FSB's Centre 16, but notably named no FSB officials — instead listing criminal actors and officials tied to the GRU, Russian military intelligence.
insights.speaker: Tom Uren
insights.so_what: The selective omission of FSB officials from sanctions despite direct attribution suggests diplomatic hedging by the EU/U.K. toward Russia's main security service — worth watching for whether follow-on sanctions target FSB directly.
insights.locator: although both announcements attribute December 2025 attacks on Poland's energy grid to the Federal Security Service

insights.claim: The European Parliament revived legal protections allowing Big Tech companies (Google, Microsoft, Meta) to voluntarily scan for and report child sexual abuse material after the prior law lapsed, with the scanning authorization for unencrypted material now set to remain in place until 2028.
insights.speaker: Tom Uren
insights.so_what: Extends legal certainty for platform CSAM-scanning programs through 2028, removing near-term compliance risk for major platforms operating in the EU on this specific issue.
insights.locator: The Record reports the European Parliament has revived legal protections for Big Tech companies

insights.claim: The White House's newly announced 'Gold Eagle' cybersecurity vulnerability disclosure clearinghouse is run out of the Treasury Department with Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent given top billing, while CISA — the agency with actual expertise in collating and disseminating vulnerability information — received only a passing mention, which the author finds an odd institutional choice.
insights.speaker: Tom Uren
insights.so_what: Sidelining CISA in favor of Treasury for vulnerability coordination is an unusual institutional design choice worth watching for effectiveness and turf-war risk in U.S. cyber policy execution.
insights.locator: This week the White House announced the launch of Gold Eagle
